note: The Catholic King of England whose reign was marked by religious tension and fears of French-style absolutism, eventually leading to the Glorious Revolution.

quote: Speaking in the zargon.
subject: Thieves' Cant
note: A secret language or cryptolect used by the criminal underworld (the 'Blacke Art') in the 16th-18th centuries to converse without being understood by the authorities.

quote: l’Emmerdeur does not need his help.
subject: L'Emmerdeur
note: A French vulgarity meaning 'the troublemaker' or 'the pain in the ass,' literally 'the shitter,' which becomes a recurring nickname for Jack Shaftoe.

quote: boots that John Churchill had donated
subject: John Churchill, 1st Duke of Marlborough
note: A prominent English soldier and statesman who rose to power under James II and later became one of the greatest generals in British history.

quote: cover the V branded on his thumb
subject: Vagabond Branding
note: The historical practice of branding criminals with letters to identify their crimes; 'V' stood for Vagabond, marking the person as a persistent beggar or homeless wanderer.

quote: Churchill had gotten some expert maréchal-ferrant to shoe him
subject: John Churchill, 1st Duke of Marlborough
note: A legendary English military commander and statesman. At this point in the narrative, he is a rising figure in the court of the Stuarts and a key associate of the Duke of York.

quote: Hôtel d’Arcachon
subject: Hôtel particulier
note: In French context, an 'hôtel' is not a public inn but a grand private townhouse for a nobleman, typically featuring a courtyard (cour) and a garden.

quote: A hundred sperm whales must have given up their bodily fluids to light the lanterns
subject: Spermaceti
note: A waxy substance found in the head cavities of sperm whales. It was the premium fuel for candles and lamps in the 17th and 18th centuries because it burned brightly and without a foul odor.

quote: fragrance of honey-scented Mauritanian beeswax
subject: Beeswax candles
note: Unlike cheap tallow candles made from animal fat, beeswax candles were expensive, burned cleanly, and smelled pleasant, marking the extreme wealth of the host.

quote: naval frigate all speckled with fleurs-de-lis
subject: Fleur-de-lis
note: The stylized lily that served as the symbol of the French monarchy. Its presence on the ship model signifies the vessel belongs to the French Royal Navy.

quote: Wreckage of Dutch and English ships washed up on shores all around
subject: Franco-Dutch War
note: The fountain depicts French naval victories over the Dutch and English during the wars of Louis XIV, reflecting the geopolitical tensions of the 1670s.

quote: little knots of Puritans, Færy-Queens, Persians, and Red Indians
subject: Masquerade Ball
note: A popular form of entertainment for the 17th-century nobility where guests dressed in 'fancy-dress' or exotic costumes, often representing allegories, different nationalities, or social classes.

quote: fish-market at Les Halles
subject: Les Halles
note: The historic central fresh food market of Paris, known for its chaotic atmosphere and the 'Forts des Halles' (strongmen) and 'Dames des Halles' (market women).

quote: clasped both hands to her black-spotted face
subject: Beauty marks (Mouches)
note: In the 17th century, artificial beauty marks made of silk or velvet (mouches) were highly fashionable and often used to hide smallpox scars or communicate flirtatious messages.

quote: Emmerdeur
subject: Emmerdeur
note: A French vulgarity roughly translating to 'troublemaker' or 'pain in the ass,' but in this context, it refers to Jack's disguise as a 'shitter' or someone covered in filth.

quote: “pour les Invalides,” then backed away curtseying
subject: Les Invalides
note: A complex of buildings in Paris commissioned by Louis XIV in 1670 as a home and hospital for aged and unwell soldiers.

quote: a certain handsome young Barbary Pirate who stood at the back of the crowd
subject: Barbary Pirates
note: Privateers and pirates who operated from North Africa (the Barbary Coast); in this context, it refers to a courtier dressed as one for a masquerade.

quote: ostrich-plume-wearing Moors
subject: Moors
note: A term used in early modern Europe to refer to Muslim inhabitants of the Maghreb, North Africa, and the Iberian Peninsula.

quote: Enemies of France... viz. English and Dutch frigates... Spanish and Portuguese galleons
subject: Enemies of France
note: Refers to the shifting alliances of the late 17th century, particularly during the Franco-Dutch War (1672–1678) where France faced a coalition of England, Spain, and the Dutch Republic.

quote: pirates of Barbary and Malta
subject: Knights of Malta
note: The Order of Malta operated a powerful navy in the Mediterranean, often engaging in state-sanctioned piracy (corsairing) against Ottoman shipping.

quote: stood a laurel-wreath-crowned Leroy
subject: Louis XIV
note: The 'Sun King' of France; 'Le Roi' (The King) is often phoneticized as 'Leroy' in Jack's perspective.

quote: one hand fingering an astrolabe
subject: Astrolabe
note: An ancient astronomical instrument used by mariners and astronomers to calculate the position of celestial bodies, essential for navigation before the sextant.

quote: noticed John Churchill striding round the back of the crowd
subject: John Churchill, 1st Duke of Marlborough
note: A prominent English soldier and statesman who served under both James II and William III; he was a key figure in the late 17th-century military landscape.

quote: examining the flintlock mechanism on a pistol
subject: Flintlock
note: A type of firearm ignition mechanism introduced in the early 17th century, replacing the matchlock and wheellock.

quote: if John Churchill—who had no sense of humor at all—had not been homing in on him
subject: John Churchill, 1st Duke of Marlborough
note: A legendary English military commander and statesman whose career spanned the reigns of five monarchs. In the novel's timeframe, he is a rising star and a formidable soldier.

quote: The East Wind had been split open... It led straight to the French Navy
subject: Baroque Ceiling Painting
note: The passage describes the collapse of an elaborate frescoed ceiling, a staple of Baroque architecture often depicting allegorical figures (like the Winds) and national triumphs (like the Navy).

quote: the flint whipping round on the end of its curved arm, a spray of sparks
subject: Flintlock Mechanism
note: The standard firearm technology of the era, which used a piece of flint striking steel to ignite gunpowder in a 'pan' to fire the weapon.

quote: Barbary pirate-galley
subject: Barbary Pirates
note: Privateers and pirates from North Africa who raided European shipping and coastal towns for cargo and slaves; they are central to the backstory of Eliza and Jack.

quote: his hard fers de cheval slipped and scrabbled on the marble
subject: Horseshoes
note: French for 'horseshoes.' The passage highlights the danger of iron shoes on polished marble floors during the chaos of the scene.

quote: a crowd of ladies who were doing a sort of tarantella
subject: Tarantella
note: A rapid folk dance from Southern Italy, historically associated with 'tarantism,' a form of hysterical dancing believed to cure the bite of a wolf spider.

quote: crevices between planks had been sealed with oakum, just like a ship’s planking
subject: Oakum
note: A preparation of loose fiber obtained by untwisting old rope, used with tar to caulk the seams of wooden ships to make them watertight.

quote: the bulwark of the Bastille rising above some rooftops
subject: The Bastille
note: A medieval fortress used as a state prison and armory; it was a dominant landmark in eastern Paris and a symbol of royal authority.

quote: JOHN DONNE, “Breake of Day”
subject: John Donne
note: A leading English poet of the Metaphysical movement and a cleric in the Church of England, known for his complex metaphors and exploration of both carnal and divine love.

quote: That’s Yevgeny the Raskolnik.
subject: Raskolnik
note: A member of the 'Old Believers' who split from the Russian Orthodox Church in the mid-17th century to protest liturgical reforms; they faced severe persecution and often fled Russia.

quote: Dunkirk’s not the same since Leroy bought it from King Chuck.
subject: Sale of Dunkirk
note: In 1662, Charles II (King Chuck) sold the strategic port of Dunkirk to Louis XIV (Leroy) for five million livres to alleviate his financial debts, a move unpopular in England.

quote: signed on with Monsieur Jean Bart
subject: Jean Bart
note: A famous French naval commander and privateer from Dunkirk who achieved great success during the wars of Louis XIV.

quote: sailed away to Port Royal, in Jamaica
subject: Port Royal
note: A notorious Caribbean port that served as a headquarters for privateers and pirates until it was largely destroyed by an earthquake in 1692.

quote: traveled in John Churchill’s retinue
subject: John Churchill, 1st Duke of Marlborough
note: A prominent English soldier and statesman whose career spanned the reigns of five monarchs; he was a key figure in the court of James II and later a hero of the War of the Spanish Succession.

quote: the Duke of Monmouth had surprised them all by coming to Dunkirk incognito
subject: Duke of Monmouth
note: The eldest illegitimate son of Charles II, who in 1685 (the year of this passage) launched a rebellion to seize the English throne from his uncle, James II.

quote: doing that binary arithmetic on her left, according to the Doctor’s practice
subject: Binary Arithmetic
note: A reference to Leibniz's development of the binary numeral system, which he viewed as a fundamental mathematical and philosophical language.

quote: doing that binary arithmetic on her left, according to the Doctor’s practice
subject: binary arithmetic
note: Leibniz (the 'Doctor') was the first to formalize the modern binary system, which he viewed as having mystical and mathematical significance.

quote: running before the trade winds
subject: Trade Winds
note: The permanent east-to-west prevailing winds that flow in the Earth's equatorial region, which were essential for sailing ships crossing the Atlantic.

quote: Dunkirk went Mr. Vliet... sell goods quickly in Dunkirk
subject: Dunkirk
note: In this era, Dunkirk was a notorious nest for privateers and smugglers, often changing hands between the Spanish, French, and English.

quote: extremely vigorous capital market right here in Amsterdam
subject: Amsterdam Stock Exchange
note: The Amsterdam Stock Exchange, established by the Dutch East India Company (VOC) in 1602, was the first modern securities market in the world.

quote: awaiting the return of John Churchill
subject: John Churchill
note: The future Duke of Marlborough and one of England's greatest generals; at this time, he was a rising courtier and military officer.

quote: Windhandel, or gifts from suitors?
subject: Windhandel
note: A Dutch term meaning 'trading in wind,' referring to speculative stock trading where the seller does not actually own the shares being traded.

quote: Huguenots and Sephardim had come to live here
subject: Huguenots and Sephardim
note: Amsterdam became a haven for French Protestants (Huguenots) fleeing persecution under Louis XIV and Sephardic Jews fleeing the Spanish and Portuguese Inquisitions.

quote: even a few Raskolniks
subject: Raskolniks
note: Members of the 'Old Believers' movement who split from the Russian Orthodox Church in the mid-17th century to protest liturgical reforms.

quote: made Mr. Vliet a loan à la grosse aventure
subject: Bottomry (Prêt à la grosse aventure)
note: A maritime contract where a shipowner borrows money for a voyage; if the ship is lost, the lender loses their money, but if it succeeds, the lender is repaid with high interest.

quote: coffee-houses that specialize in just that
subject: Marine Insurance
note: Early insurance markets, such as Lloyd's of London, began in coffeehouses where merchants and underwriters gathered to share news and trade risks.

quote: doubting Thomas
subject: Doubting Thomas
note: A biblical reference to the Apostle Thomas, who refused to believe Jesus had been resurrected until he could touch the wounds; used here to describe Eliza's skepticism and physical verification of the cargo.

quote: Cowrie shells... In Africa, this is money!
subject: Cowrie Shells as Currency
note: Small sea shells used for centuries as a global commodity money, particularly in the slave trade between Europe, Africa, and southern Asia.

quote: Dutch had acquired certain isles, near India, called the Maldives and Laccadives
subject: Dutch East India Company in the Maldives
note: The Dutch East India Company (VOC) sought to monopolize the cowrie shell trade in the Maldives to control the 'currency' used to purchase slaves in West Africa.

quote: Now I understand the word Windhandel in a new way
subject: Windhandel
note: A Dutch term meaning 'trade in wind,' referring to speculative trading in stocks or commodities that the trader does not actually possess, often associated with the Tulip Mania and early stock markets.

quote: round the Cape of Good Hope
subject: Cape of Good Hope
note: The southern tip of Africa; the primary maritime route for European trade with Asia before the Suez Canal, known for its treacherous waters.

quote: exchange our cowrie-shells for pieces of India
subject: Piece of India (Pieza de India)
note: A unit of value used in the trans-Atlantic slave trade, originally referring to a 'standard' healthy male slave against which other 'goods' were measured.

quote: You are going into the slave trade
subject: Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade
note: The historical triangular trade involving the transport of enslaved Africans to the Americas, which was a central pillar of the 17th and 18th-century global economy.

quote: Imp of the Perverse
subject: The Imp of the Perverse
note: A metaphor for the human urge to do the wrong thing specifically because it is wrong or self-destructive (later popularized by Edgar Allan Poe).

quote: looted from the Grand Vizier’s camp
subject: Battle of Vienna (1683)
note: The historical event where Jack and Eliza met; the defeat of the Ottoman Empire's forces led by Grand Vizier Kara Mustafa, resulting in immense loot for the victors.

quote: outlandish Raskolnik priest in a towering hat
subject: Raskolnik (Old Believers)
note: Members of the 'Old Believers' movement who split from the Russian Orthodox Church in the mid-17th century in protest against liturgical reforms.

quote: the comte d’Avaux and he kissed hers
subject: Jean-Antoine de Mesmes, Comte d'Avaux
note: A key French diplomat and ambassador to the Dutch Republic during the late 17th century, known for his efforts to undermine William of Orange and promote French interests.

quote: the invasion fleet that sailed for England, from Texel, the other day, flying the Duke of Monmouth’s colors
subject: Duke of Monmouth
note: References the start of the Monmouth Rebellion in 1685, where the Duke of Monmouth attempted to overthrow his uncle, James II, by landing a fleet from the Netherlands.

quote: the feral mob of Orangist patriots
subject: Orangism
note: A political faction in the Dutch Republic that supported the House of Orange-Nassau (specifically William III) against the 'States' faction of wealthy merchants.

quote: This kaag will take you
subject: Kaag (ship)
note: A traditional Dutch shallow-draft sailing vessel used for inland navigation on canals and lakes, characterized by its distinctive curved hull and leeboards.

quote: through the Exchange, which was as good a place as any to lose pursuers
subject: Amsterdam Stock Exchange
note: The Beurs van Hendrick de Keyser, the world's first formal stock exchange, which was the center of global finance and speculation in the 17th century.

quote: It was up to the drost to interrogate the very few witnesses
subject: Drost
note: A Dutch administrative official or bailiff responsible for maintaining order and justice in a specific district.

quote: recent crash of V.O.C. stock
subject: Dutch East India Company (VOC)
note: The Vereenigde Oost-Indische Compagnie, the first multinational corporation, whose stock was the primary driver of the Amsterdam financial markets.

quote: The Duke of Monmouth’s rebellion has made lead a valuable commodity again
subject: Duke of Monmouth
note: James Scott, 1st Duke of Monmouth, was the illegitimate son of Charles II who led a 1685 rebellion against his uncle, James II, in an attempt to seize the English throne.

quote: Arnold Joost van Keppel,” he explained curtly, “page to the Prince of Orange.
subject: Arnold Joost van Keppel
note: A close confidant and rumored lover of William of Orange, Keppel later became the 1st Earl of Albemarle and a powerful figure in both Dutch and English politics.

quote: William, Prince of Orange, Stadholder, Admiral-General, and Captain-General of the United Provinces
subject: James II
note: William III of Orange was the sovereign Prince of Orange and Stadtholder of the Dutch Republic who eventually took the English throne during the Glorious Revolution.

quote: As d’Avaux never got tired of pointing out
subject: Jean-Antoine de Mesmes, Comte d'Avaux
note: A veteran French diplomat and ambassador to the Dutch Republic who worked tirelessly to advance Louis XIV's interests and undermine William of Orange.

quote: You are acquainted with Doctor Leibniz?
subject: Gottfried Wilhelm von Leibniz
note: A polymath and philosopher who served the House of Hanover; he is a central figure in the novel's scientific and political subplots.

quote: save that the Elector has a palace there.
subject: Frederick William, Elector of Brandenburg
note: Known as the 'Great Elector,' he was the ruler of Brandenburg-Prussia and a key player in the shifting European alliances against Louis XIV.

quote: In fact, the Regents of Amsterdam were plotting with your friend d’Avaux
subject: Regents of Amsterdam
note: The 'Regenten' were the merchant-oligarchs who ruled Dutch cities; they often clashed with the House of Orange over trade interests and peace with France.

quote: William announced. “I was pleased to have fared so well, and Louis was furious to have made out so poorly.”
subject: William III of Orange
note: The Stadtholder of the Dutch Republic and future King of England, Scotland, and Ireland. He was the primary antagonist of Louis XIV's expansionist policies in Europe.

quote: Louis is not like us—he does not trifle with reasons. He is a reason.
subject: Louis XIV
note: The 'Sun King' of France, whose absolute monarchy and desire for 'Universal Monarchy' made him the central political force of the era.

quote: for fear of French dragoons
subject: Dragoons
note: Mounted infantry used by Louis XIV to suppress dissent, most notably in the 'Dragonnades' against French Protestants (Huguenots).

quote: Louis wants to enslave all of Christendom.
subject: Universal Monarchy
note: The contemporary political fear that one monarch (Louis XIV) would achieve total hegemony over Europe, effectively ending the sovereignty of other nations.

quote: d’Avaux has assassins all over the city.
subject: Jean-Antoine de Mesmes, Comte d'Avaux
note: A key French diplomat and spy-master who served as Louis XIV's ambassador to the Dutch Republic.

quote: your lover’s invasion of England has imposed certain obligations on me.
subject: Monmouth Rebellion
note: The 1685 attempt by the Duke of Monmouth to overthrow his uncle, James II, which put William of Orange in a delicate political position regarding his own claim to the throne.

quote: Arnold! ... Arnold Joost van Keppel.
subject: Arnold Joost van Keppel
note: A close confidant and alleged favorite of William of Orange, later created the 1st Earl of Albemarle.

quote: Doctor Leibniz has taught you cyphers
subject: Gottfried Wilhelm von Leibniz
note: The polymath philosopher and mathematician who, in the novel, acts as a mentor to Eliza and has a historical interest in cryptography.

quote: spy for you but for Sophie
subject: Sophia of the Palatinate
note: The Electress of Hanover and a key intellectual figure who was next in line for the English throne after the Stuart heirs.

quote: but he’s no John Churchill.
subject: John Churchill, 1st Duke of Marlborough
note: One of England's greatest military commanders who would eventually lead the Grand Alliance against Louis XIV.

quote: overthrow King James, do you?
subject: James II
note: The Catholic King of England whose reign was marked by religious tension and the eventual Glorious Revolution.

quote: since he must have known what Monmouth was planning
subject: Duke of Monmouth
note: James Scott, 1st Duke of Monmouth, was the eldest illegitimate son of Charles II who led a rebellion to seize the English throne from his Catholic uncle, James II.

quote: Monmouth has landed, of all places, in Dorset—John Churchill’s home ground!
subject: John Churchill, 1st Duke of Marlborough
note: A brilliant military commander and statesman who served multiple monarchs; he was instrumental in defeating the Monmouth Rebellion before later defecting to William of Orange.

quote: In order to defeat Louis, I’ll need Britain.
subject: Louis XIV
note: The 'Sun King' of France, whose expansionist policies and Catholicism made him the primary antagonist to William of Orange and the Protestant powers of Europe.

quote: fell to her knees in front of the Prince of Orange
subject: William III of England (William of Orange)
note: The Sovereign Prince of Orange and Stadtholder of the Dutch Republic who eventually took the English throne during the Glorious Revolution.

quote: you learned this and many other practices in the Harim of the Sultan
subject: Imperial Harem of the Ottoman Empire
note: The secluded quarters of the Sultan's household in Constantinople; Eliza's backstory involves being captured by corsairs and sold into this system.

quote: John Donne, “Elegie XX: Loves Warre”
subject: John Donne
note: A leading English poet of the late 16th and early 17th centuries, known for his metaphysical style and complex metaphors.
